attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents with a six day history of worsening chest pain, aggravated by deep breathing and coughing. He has a dry cough. He also has headaches, muscle aches and nausea. He is otherwise in good health, does not take any medications, does not smoke and drinks a few cans of beer at weekends. He is a dairy farmer and has not done any recent travelling.

On examination, his blood pressure is 120/88 mmHg, pulse 80/min, temperature 39.4°C and respiratory rate 18/min. He has several enlarged and tender cervical lymph nodes, with no rash. His chest is clear and his heart sounds are dual. His blood results are shown:

  • Hb: 133g/L (130 – 180)

  • WCC: 14.0x 109/L (3.6 – 11.0)

  • Platelets 118x109/L (140 – 400)

  • C-RP: 7mg/L (< 5)

  • Bilirubin: 18μmol/L (<21)

  • Aspartate aminotransferase: 88U/L (1 – 45)

  • Alanine aminotransferase: 96U/L (<41)

  • Alkaline phosphatase: 156U/L (30–130)

  • GGT: 97U/L (<60)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is infected with Coxiella burnetii, the causative microbe for Q fever. Working on farms, particularly with cows, an important risk factor for this infection. Clinical features include fever, nausea, headache, muscle aches, vomiting and diarrhoea. Examination findings include lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. It is also often associated with hepatitis (and this patient's liver function tests show a transaminitis) and is also an important cause of atypical pneumonia. The Weil Felix test can help diagnose Q fever - no clumping indicates that the patient has Coxiella burnetii infection. This is followed with confirmatory PCR testing on blood. The most appropriate treatment for Q fever is doxycycline. Current guidelines state that an acute infection of Q fever requires 14 days of treatment with doxycycline.

Azithromycin would be recommended if the causative microbe of infection was Chlamydophila pneumoniae or Legionella pneumophila. However, as indicated by the risk factor of working with cows and the hepatitis, infection by these microbes is less likely.

Acyclovir would be recommended if the cause of infection was a viral cause such as Herpes simplex virus. However, based on the associated risk factors, signs and symptoms this is less likely to be these viral infections. Note, this is also less likely to be Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) because while EBV can cause cervical lymphadenopathy and hepatitis, it is less likely to be a source of pneumonia, and is less likely than Q fever based on the symptoms and associated risk factors. Furthermore, it is explicitly noted that there is no rash (which can occur in EBV infection). Note also, acyclovir would not be used for EBV anyways - treatment would just be done for symptoms in EBV, such as corticosteroids.

Ceftriaxone would not be recommended for this patient as this antibiotic is broad-spectrum, and not necessary for this patient who is most likely to have Q fever.

Piperacillin/tazobactam may be recommended if the patient is infected with Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which is far less likely here.
learningPoints: Being a farmer is an important risk factor for Coxiella burneti, which is the cause of Q fever
Doxycycline for 14 days is the recommended form of treatment for acute infection of Q fever.
references: Cherry, C. C., Nichols Heitman, K., Bestul, N. C., & Kersh, G. J. (2022). Acute and chronic Q fever national surveillance–United States, 2008–2017. Zoonoses and Public Health, 69(2), 73-82.
Eastwood, K., Graves, S. R., Massey, P. D., Bosward, K., & Hutchinson, P. (2018). Q fever: A rural disease with potential urban consequences. Australian Journal of General Practice, 47(3), 112-116.

Garin, N., Marti, C., Skali Lami, A., & Prendki, V. (2022). Atypical Pathogens in Adult Community-Acquired Pneumonia and Implications for Empiric Antibiotic Treatment: A Narrative Review. Microorganisms, 10(12), 2326.

Hoover K, Higginbotham K. Epstein-Barr Virus. [Updated 2022 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559285/
